h1Title: Idiopathic Intracranial Hypertension: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Idiopathic intracranial hypertension (IIH) is a syndrome of raised intracranial pressure without an identifiable cause — no tumour, hydrocephalus, venous thrombosis, or metabolic abnormality — producing chronic daily headache, papilloedema, and progressive visual loss in the absence of an underlying structural brain lesion. The incidence is approximately 1–2 per 100,000 per year overall, rising to 20–25 per 100,000 in obese women of reproductive age — the group at highest risk. Untreated, IIH threatens permanent visual loss through optic nerve compression, occurring in up to 25% of patients. Diagnosis requires lumbar puncture demonstrating CSF opening pressure above 25 cmH2O in adults. Weight reduction of 15% body weight produces sustained remission in most patients, and acetazolamide reduces CSF production as the key pharmacological intervention.

keyStats.prevalence: Incidence 1–2 per 100,000 per year overall; 20–25 per 100,000 in obese women aged 15–44 (Mollan et al. 2018, J Neurol Neurosurg Psychiatry)
keyStats.demographics: Over 90% of IIH patients are female; 90–94% are obese (BMI >30 kg/m²); peak incidence at ages 20–44 years
keyStats.avgAge: Mean age at diagnosis 28–32 years; bimodal distribution with a smaller peak in post-menopausal women
keyStats.globalCases: IIH rates have approximately doubled in the UK and US over the past 20 years, parallel to rising obesity prevalence; estimated 50,000 active cases in the UK

definition: Idiopathic intracranial hypertension (ICD-10: G93.2), formerly known as pseudotumor cerebri or benign intracranial hypertension, is a condition of sustained elevation of intracranial pressure (ICP) above 25 cmH2O (measured by lumbar puncture in a relaxed lateral decubitus position) that occurs without evidence of an underlying cause on standard neuroimaging and laboratory investigation. The Friedman and Jacobson criteria (2002) define IIH as: (1) symptoms and signs of raised ICP (headache, papilloedema, visual disturbance), (2) CSF opening pressure above 25 cmH2O, (3) normal CSF composition, (4) no identifiable structural, vascular, or metabolic cause, and (5) no meningism. The pathophysiology is incompletely understood but involves disrupted CSF absorption at the arachnoid villi — possibly driven by increased abdominal pressure in obesity transmitting to intracranial venous pressure — with emerging evidence for elevated androgen-driven CSF secretion from the choroid plexus. Neurologists, neuro-ophthalmologists, and neurosurgeons collaborate on management. The term 'benign' has been abandoned because the condition causes permanent visual loss in up to 25% of patients without adequate treatment.

typesClassification.description: The classical presentation: obese woman of reproductive age with headache, bilateral papilloedema, transient visual obscurations, and CSF pressure above 25 cmH2O. Risk of visual loss is significant and monitoring of visual fields at every visit is mandatory.

Elevated ICP without visible papilloedema, occurring in approximately 10% of IIH cases. Presents with headache and pulsatile tinnitus. Diagnosis requires raised CSF pressure plus additional criteria (transverse venous sinus stenosis, enlarged Meckel's cave, empty sella on MRI). Visual loss risk is lower than typical IIH.

Rapid-onset severe IIH causing profound visual loss within weeks of symptom onset. Requires emergency neurosurgical intervention (optic nerve sheath fenestration or CSF diversion) within days. Accounts for approximately 2–5% of IIH cases. Associated with rapid weight gain, pregnancy, and anaemia.

primarySymptoms: Chronic daily headache, the most common symptom (in 85–94% of patients), typically pulsatile, bifrontal or holocephalic, worse in the morning, with activity, Valsalva manoeuvres, and lying down — reflecting positional changes in ICP.

Transient visual obscurations (TVOs): brief episodes of greying or blurring of vision in one or both eyes lasting seconds, often triggered by changes in posture, bending, or coughing — highly specific for raised ICP with papilloedema.

Bilateral papilloedema visible on fundoscopy: disc swelling, blurred disc margins, and obscuration of retinal veins reflecting raised ICP; may be asymptomatic or associated with progressive visual field loss.

Pulsatile intracranial tinnitus: a whooshing, rhythmic sound synchronous with the heartbeat heard by the patient (approximately 60% of cases), generated by turbulent blood flow in venous sinuses under high pressure.

Visual field defects: arcuate scotomata, enlarged blind spot, and constriction of the peripheral visual field detected on formal perimetry — the objective measure of optic nerve damage and the key outcome for treatment decisions.

Binocular horizontal diplopia from a non-localising sixth nerve (abducens) palsy, occurring in approximately 20–30% of patients due to raised ICP causing downward displacement of the brain and stretching of the sixth cranial nerve.
Neck, back, or radicular pain from raised CSF pressure in the spinal canal, a feature particularly prominent when the patient is recumbent.
Cognitive fog, difficulty concentrating, and memory complaints, reported by 60–70% of patients in quality-of-life studies.
Photophobia and phonophobia, often leading to an erroneous diagnosis of migraine in early or untreated IIH.
earlyWarningSigns: Persistent daily headache with a pulsatile quality in an obese woman of reproductive age — while migraine is more common, IIH must be excluded when headache is new, daily, and positional.

Pulsatile tinnitus described as a whooshing sound synchronous with the heartbeat, especially when heard with only one ear in a young woman — this specific symptom should prompt fundoscopy at the first opportunity.
Transient visual obscurations lasting seconds, especially triggered by posture change — a fairly specific warning sign of papilloedema and raised ICP.
An enlarged blind spot on confrontational visual field testing in a patient with headache and obesity — warrant urgent referral for formal perimetry and fundoscopy.
emergencySigns: Rapid onset or worsening of visual loss over days in a known IIH patient — fulminant IIH; requires same-day ophthalmology review, IV acetazolamide, and neurosurgical assessment for emergency optic nerve sheath fenestration or LP.
Loss of visual acuity to 6/60 or worse in one or both eyes in any IIH patient — irreversible optic nerve damage is imminent; emergency intervention is required.
Severe new papilloedema discovered incidentally in a patient without previous IIH — requires same-day CT to exclude structural cause and lumbar puncture if CT is clear.
Sixth nerve palsy with headache and papilloedema in a new presentation — raises ICP needs urgent imaging and LP to exclude intracranial mass or venous sinus thrombosis.
Grade IV or V papilloedema (haemorrhages and exudates around the disc) — indicates severely raised ICP threatening rapid visual loss; same-day neurology or neurosurgery referral.

causes.description: Obesity (BMI above 30 kg/m²) is present in 90–94% of IIH cases. The mechanism may involve elevated intra-abdominal pressure transmitting via the epidural venous plexus to raise intracranial venous pressure and impair CSF reabsorption at arachnoid granulations. Weight gain of as little as 5–15% increases IIH risk; conversely, 15% weight loss produces remission in most patients. The IIH Treatment Trial (IIHTT) confirmed weight loss as the most effective long-term treatment.

The >90% female predominance and peak in reproductive-age women points to sex hormone involvement. Androgens stimulate the choroid plexus via 11β-HSD1, increasing CSF production. Elevated circulating androgen levels and altered glucocorticoid metabolism have been measured in IIH compared with matched obese controls. Oral contraceptive pills, pregnancy, and polycystic ovary syndrome are associated with IIH, though causality is not firmly established.

Bilateral transverse venous sinus stenosis is detected on MR venography or CT venography in approximately 90% of IIH patients and appears to be a consequence rather than a primary cause — raised ICP compresses the sinus walls, creating stenosis that further impairs venous drainage. The resulting venous hypertension impairs CSF reabsorption, creating a self-sustaining pressure cycle. Venous sinus stenting reverses this cycle in selected patients.

Several medications cause a syndrome indistinguishable from IIH: tetracyclines (especially minocycline and doxycycline for acne — possibly the most common drug trigger), high-dose vitamin A and retinoids (isotretinoin), corticosteroid withdrawal, levothyroxine over-replacement, and lithium. These are classified as secondary intracranial hypertension, not true IIH, but management is similar. Stopping the offending drug often produces rapid remission.

Thrombosis of the dural venous sinuses produces raised ICP by obstructing venous drainage and CSF reabsorption. It is distinguished from IIH by MR venography, which shows absent flow in the affected sinus. Cerebral venous sinus thrombosis requires anticoagulation; IIH does not. Failure to exclude CVST is a potentially fatal diagnostic error.

Severe iron-deficiency anaemia, particularly in menstruating women with heavy periods, has been associated with IIH — possibly through reduced blood viscosity altering cerebral venous haemodynamics. Correcting anaemia can improve IIH symptoms and is part of the routine investigation and management.

riskFactors.description: Women are affected approximately 10–15 times more frequently than men. The sex disparity is closely linked to reproductive hormones and adiposity distribution in women of reproductive age.

Present in 90–94% of patients with IIH. Risk correlates with BMI — each 1 kg/m² increase in BMI raises IIH risk by approximately 15%. Recent weight gain (above 5% body weight) is a particularly important precipitant.

Peak incidence coincides with reproductive years in women, supporting a role for sex hormones. IIH in children and post-menopausal women is less obesity-dependent and more likely to be drug-induced or idiopathic.

Tetracycline antibiotics, widely used for acne vulgaris, are among the most commonly identified drug triggers of secondary IIH. The mechanism may involve disruption of CSF reabsorption. Stopping the drug typically produces remission.
High-dose vitamin A derivatives cause raised ICP, presumably through effects on CSF secretion and venous sinus function. IIH symptoms warrant immediate cessation of isotretinoin.

PCOS, with its associated hyperandrogenism and insulin resistance, increases IIH risk — possibly through elevated androgen stimulation of choroid plexus secretion. Approximately 20–30% of women with IIH have concurrent PCOS.
Iron-deficiency anaemia — from heavy menstrual bleeding — is overrepresented in IIH. Treating anaemia contributes to overall management and may reduce CSF pressure.

affectedDemographics: Obese women aged 20–44 account for over 80% of IIH cases in high-income countries; incidence in this group is 20–25 per 100,000 per year.
IIH incidence has doubled in the UK and US over the past two decades, mirroring the rise in obesity prevalence, particularly in women of reproductive age.
Post-menopausal women and men represent approximately 10% of cases each; in these groups, drug-induced causes, sleep apnoea, and systemic diseases are more likely to be responsible.
Children and adolescents can develop IIH; in prepubertal children there is no sex predilection, and obesity is less consistently associated — drug-induced and secondary causes are more common.
IIH is more common in Black women than white women in US epidemiological studies, possibly related to higher prevalence of obesity and differences in adiposity distribution.
diagnosisOverview: IIH is diagnosed by exclusion. The MODIFY criteria (2019) require: (1) lumbar puncture opening pressure above 25 cmH2O in adults (above 28 cmH2O in children) in the lateral decubitus position; (2) normal CSF composition — any pleocytosis, elevated protein, or low glucose requires an alternative diagnosis; (3) neuroimaging (MRI brain with gadolinium and MR venography) showing no structural, hydrocephalic, or vascular cause; and (4) symptoms or signs of raised ICP. The MRI signature of IIH includes: empty sella (cerebrospinal fluid herniation into the pituitary fossa), bilateral transverse sinus stenosis, distension of the optic nerve sheath with vertical tortuosity, flattening of the posterior sclera, and protrusion of the optic disc head. These are not diagnostic but are highly supportive. Fundoscopic examination by a skilled ophthalmologist identifies papilloedema and grades its severity (Frisén scale grades 1–5). Formal automated Humphrey visual fields with a 24-2 threshold programme are mandatory at diagnosis and at every review appointment. CSF constituents (cell count, protein, glucose, cytology) must be sent at every diagnostic LP. Blood investigations include FBC (anaemia), ferritin, sex hormones, cortisol (excluding Cushing's), thyroid function, and vitamin A level. Venous sinus thrombosis must be excluded on MRV before accepting an IIH diagnosis — failure to do so is a potentially fatal omission.

diagnosticTests.test: Lumbar puncture with opening pressure measurement
diagnosticTests.purpose: Gold-standard diagnostic test; opening pressure above 25 cmH2O in adults confirms raised ICP; CSF composition must be normal to support IIH diagnosis
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The patient lies curled on their side. After local anaesthetic, a manometer is attached to the LP needle to measure CSF pressure before any fluid is withdrawn. Takes 20–30 minutes. The pressure reading is the most important number. CSF is also collected for cell count, protein, glucose, and cytology.

diagnosticTests.test: MRI brain with gadolinium and MR venography (MRV)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes structural causes (tumour, hydrocephalus, cerebral venous sinus thrombosis) and identifies IIH-associated radiological features: empty sella, optic nerve sheath distension, transverse sinus stenosis
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard MRI with contrast dye injected via IV. Takes 45–60 minutes. Specific venography sequences assess flow in the dural venous sinuses. Results reviewed by a neuroradiologist within 24 hours.

diagnosticTests.test: Fundoscopy and Frisén grading of papilloedema
diagnosticTests.purpose: Documents severity of optic disc swelling, which directly reflects ICP and guides urgency of treatment; Frisén scale grades 1 (subtle) to 5 (severe with haemorrhages)
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Ophthalmologist examines the optic disc through a dilated pupil with a slit lamp or indirect ophthalmoscope. Retinal photography documents the degree of swelling for comparison at follow-up.

diagnosticTests.test: Formal automated visual field testing (Humphrey 24-2 threshold)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects and quantifies visual field loss from optic nerve compression; the primary clinical outcome measure in IIH treatment decisions
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Patient sits at the visual field perimeter and presses a button when they see a light stimulus. Takes 10–15 minutes per eye. Reliable results require patient cooperation. Repeated at every follow-up visit — progressive field loss despite treatment is the main indication for surgical escalation.

diagnosticTests.test: Optical coherence tomography (OCT) of retinal nerve fibre layer (RNFL)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies peripapillary RNFL thickness, allowing objective tracking of disc swelling and early detection of optic nerve atrophy
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Non-contact laser scan of the optic disc. Takes 2–3 minutes per eye. Produces a numerical thickness value that can be compared over time. RNFL thinning indicates optic nerve damage and drives treatment escalation.

diagnosticTests.test: Blood investigations: FBC, ferritin, sex hormones, TSH, cortisol, vitamin A level
diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes secondary causes of raised ICP (anaemia, thyroid dysfunction, Cushing's, vitamin A toxicity) and identifies modifiable risk factors
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard fasting blood draw. Results within 24 hours for most parameters; hormone levels may take 3–5 days. Ferritin should be assessed given the association between iron-deficiency anaemia and IIH in menstruating women.

treatmentOverview: The IIH Treatment Trial (IIHTT, 2014) established acetazolamide 2,000 mg/day plus dietary weight loss as the evidence-based first-line combination treatment. Weight loss of 15% body weight produces IIH remission in up to 25% of patients at one year and is the most effective long-term intervention; even 5–10% weight loss reduces ICP and improves symptoms. Dietary counselling and, where indicated, bariatric surgery are key components of management. Acetazolamide inhibits carbonic anhydrase, reducing choroid plexus CSF production by approximately 50%. The IIHTT demonstrated that acetazolamide 2,000 mg/day (titrated up from 500 mg daily to minimise side effects) produced significantly greater improvement in papilloedema grade and visual field mean deviation than diet alone. Topiramate 100–200 mg daily is an alternative for patients intolerant of acetazolamide — it reduces CSF production and promotes weight loss. Serial therapeutic lumbar punctures are used as a bridging strategy in fulminant presentations while awaiting surgical intervention or weight loss effect; they provide temporary relief lasting days to weeks and are not a long-term strategy. Intracranial pressure monitoring and CSF diversion surgery — ventriculoperitoneal (VP) shunt or lumboperitoneal (LP) shunt — are indicated when visual loss progresses despite maximum medical therapy. Optic nerve sheath fenestration (ONSF) surgically decompresses the optic nerve and is highly effective at halting visual field loss; it does not reduce headache or ICP. Endovascular transverse venous sinus stenting is an emerging treatment for patients with significant sinus stenosis: it reduces CSF pressure, improves papilloedema, and provides headache relief in 70–80% of carefully selected patients.

medicalTreatments.name: Acetazolamide (start 250–500 mg twice daily; target 2,000 mg/day in divided doses)
medicalTreatments.description: Carbonic anhydrase inhibitor that reduces CSF production by approximately 50%. Started low and uptitrated weekly to minimise side effects (paraesthesiae, dysgeusia, polyuria). Contraindicated in sulfonamide allergy, severe renal impairment, and hypokalaemia. Monitor electrolytes and renal function.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: IIHTT (2014, PMID 24695747): acetazolamide 2,000 mg/day plus diet produced greater papilloedema grade reduction and visual field improvement than diet alone at 6 months; NNT ≈ 5 for meaningful visual field improvement

medicalTreatments.name: Dietary weight loss programme (target ≥15% body weight reduction)
medicalTreatments.description: Structured low-calorie dietary programme aiming for 15% weight loss. The IIHTT confirmed weight loss as the most influential modifier of IIH course. Bariatric surgery is indicated for patients unable to achieve weight loss through diet alone; it produces sustained remission in 75–90% of post-operative IIH patients.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: 15% weight loss produces IIH remission in approximately 25% at 12 months in the IIHTT; bariatric surgery produces remission in 75–90% of patients in observational series

medicalTreatments.name: Topiramate (start 25 mg daily; target 100–200 mg daily in divided doses)
medicalTreatments.description: Carbonic anhydrase inhibitor with anti-migraine properties. Second-line alternative when acetazolamide is not tolerated. The weight-loss side effect of topiramate is an additional benefit in IIH. Monitor for cognitive side effects, kidney stones, and metabolic acidosis.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Observational series and small RCTs suggest comparable papilloedema reduction to acetazolamide at equivalent doses; weight loss of 3–5% in 6 months is a consistent additional benefit

medicalTreatments.name: Therapeutic lumbar puncture (10–20 mL CSF removed per session)
medicalTreatments.description: Provides rapid ICP relief in acute, fulminant, or peri-operative settings. Not a long-term treatment because ICP returns within days to weeks. Useful as a bridge to surgery or during pregnancy when medications are limited.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Immediate ICP reduction and symptomatic relief in nearly all patients; relief duration 1–6 weeks; repeated LP over months has complication risks (post-LP headache, infection, needle-track fistula)

medicalTreatments.name: Furosemide (20–40 mg daily)
medicalTreatments.description: Weak carbonic anhydrase inhibitor and diuretic used as an adjunct to acetazolamide or when acetazolamide is contraindicated at full dose. Also reduces CSF production via a separate mechanism. Rarely used as monotherapy.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Modest CSF pressure reduction; insufficient evidence from RCTs to quantify benefit independently of acetazolamide

surgicalOptions.name: Optic nerve sheath fenestration (ONSF)
surgicalOptions.description: Small incisions made in the orbital optic nerve sheath to decompress the nerve and halt progressive visual field loss. Highly effective at protecting vision but does not reduce headache or global ICP. Performed by specialist oculoplastic or orbital surgeons.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Visual stabilisation or improvement in approximately 85–90% of patients; some risk of diplopia (5–10%) from orbital fat manipulation

surgicalOptions.name: Ventriculoperitoneal (VP) or lumboperitoneal (LP) shunt
surgicalOptions.description: CSF diversion from the ventricles (VP) or lumbar subarachnoid space (LP) into the peritoneal cavity. Reduces global ICP and improves headache and papilloedema. LP shunts have higher revision rates (40–60% at 2 years); VP shunts require neuronavigation but are more durable.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Papilloedema resolves in 75–85% of patients; headache improves in 70–80%; revision surgery required in 40–60% of LP shunts over 5 years

surgicalOptions.name: Endovascular venous sinus stenting
surgicalOptions.description: Stent placement in the stenotic transverse sinus under fluoroscopic guidance. Reduces CSF pressure, resolves papilloedema, and improves headache in 70–80% of selected patients with significant pressure gradients across the sinus stenosis. Requires dual antiplatelet therapy for 3–6 months post-procedure.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Technical success rate over 98%; symptomatic improvement in 70–80%; complication rate (haemorrhage, thrombosis) approximately 2–4% in experienced centres

alternativeTreatments.name: Glucagon-like peptide-1 receptor agonist therapy (semaglutide, liraglutide)
alternativeTreatments.description: GLP-1 agonists produce 15–20% body weight reduction and are being studied in IIH as adjuncts to dietary weight loss. Early trial data (ISALT study) suggest ICP reduction correlating with weight loss. Not yet approved for IIH specifically but increasingly used off-label in obese IIH patients who cannot sustain dietary weight loss.

alternativeTreatments.name: Aquaporin-4 modulation
alternativeTreatments.description: Experimental area of research: aquaporin-4 water channels regulate CSF movement across ependymal and arachnoidal membranes. Animal model evidence suggests aquaporin-4 upregulation in obese IIH, but no approved AQP4-targeting treatments are available for clinical use yet.

whySeeSpecialist: All patients with confirmed or suspected IIH should be seen by a neurologist and neuro-ophthalmologist. The neuro-ophthalmologist monitors visual field testing and papilloedema grading — the key clinical outcomes — while the neurologist manages medications and surgical referral. Any worsening vision warrants same-day emergency review.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek a neurologist with expertise in intracranial pressure disorders or a neuro-ophthalmologist at a centre that performs formal visual field testing at every visit. For surgical consideration, choose a neurosurgeon experienced in CSF shunting with low revision rates, or an interventional neuroradiologist offering venous sinus stenting at high-volume centres.
hospitalCriteria: MRI brain with gadolinium and MR venography for diagnostic workup
Ophthalmology unit performing Humphrey automated visual field testing and OCT
Neuro-ophthalmology or neurology outpatient clinic for regular monitoring
Neurosurgery service with experience in VP and LP shunting
Interventional neuroradiology for venous sinus stenting
keyFacilities: Neuro-ophthalmology clinic with visual field perimetry and OCT
Ophthalmology retinal photography unit for papilloedema documentation
Bariatric surgery service for eligible patients
preventionStrategies: Maintain a healthy body weight (BMI below 25 kg/m²) — obesity is the single most important modifiable risk factor for IIH in women of reproductive age.
Avoid prolonged use of tetracycline antibiotics (minocycline, doxycycline) for acne where alternatives are available — tetracyclines are the most commonly implicated drug triggers of secondary IIH.
Use isotretinoin with awareness of IIH risk; report new headache or visual disturbance immediately when on this drug.
Treat heavy menstrual bleeding promptly to prevent iron-deficiency anaemia, which is associated with IIH susceptibility.
Report new pulsatile tinnitus, daily headache, or brief visual obscurations to a physician promptly — early diagnosis allows treatment before significant optic nerve damage occurs.
lifestyleModifications: Commit to a structured dietary weight-loss programme aiming for at least 15% body weight reduction — this is the most effective long-term treatment and may produce full remission.
Take acetazolamide at the prescribed dose consistently; paraesthesiae (tingling in fingers and toes) are very common but not harmful and usually improve after weeks.
Drink fluids to ensure adequate hydration when taking diuretics; electrolyte monitoring (potassium, bicarbonate) every 3 months on acetazolamide.
Attend visual field testing at every scheduled clinic visit — missed tests mean missed early visual loss.
Sleep with the head of the bed elevated 30–45 degrees to reduce supine ICP.
Limit dietary vitamin A and avoid vitamin A supplements, as excess vitamin A can elevate ICP.

recommended: Low-calorie, high-protein diet (1,000–1,500 kcal/day) under dietitian supervision to achieve 15% body weight reduction
High-fibre vegetables and legumes to support satiety and metabolic health
Low-sodium foods (below 5 g/day) to reduce fluid retention and lower blood pressure
Potassium-rich foods (bananas, avocados, spinach) to counteract the hypokalaemic effect of acetazolamide
avoid: Vitamin A-rich supplements and high-dose retinol supplements, which can raise ICP
Ultra-processed, high-calorie, nutrient-poor foods that impede weight loss
High-sodium foods that worsen fluid retention
Alcohol in excess — no specific IIH data but general metabolic and BMI impact

exerciseGuidelines: Regular aerobic exercise is strongly encouraged as part of weight management. Walking 150 minutes per week is the minimum recommended level. Higher-intensity exercise (cycling, swimming) is safe and produces greater weight loss. Avoid vigorous Valsalva manoeuvres during exercise (heavy lifting, straining) as these transiently elevate ICP in the acute phase when papilloedema is severe.
dailyManagement: Take acetazolamide in divided doses with food to reduce gastrointestinal side effects; do not skip doses.
Record daily symptoms: headache severity (0–10), duration, and any visual changes in a diary to share with the neurology team.
Perform the daily head-of-bed elevation test: if morning headaches are more severe than evening headaches, ensure sleeping position is at 30 degrees.
Attend ophthalmology visual field and OCT appointments without fail — clinical course of optic nerve damage is only visible on these tests.
Monitor weight weekly and track progress toward the 15% weight-loss target.
Report any sudden worsening of vision, new diplopia, or severe vomiting immediately to the IIH team or emergency department.
prognosis: The prognosis of IIH depends critically on early diagnosis and treatment adherence. Permanent visual loss, the most feared complication, occurs in approximately 10–25% of patients, predominantly through progressive optic nerve atrophy from chronic papilloedema. Early and aggressive treatment — weight loss, acetazolamide, and surgical intervention when visual fields deteriorate — substantially reduces this risk. IIH frequently behaves as a relapsing-remitting condition: approximately 50% of patients achieve remission (particularly with sustained weight loss) but 20–25% relapse, particularly with weight regain. Headache often persists as the most disabling long-term symptom, even when papilloedema has resolved, and may require migraine prophylaxis agents in addition to ICP-lowering therapy. Pulsatile tinnitus resolves in approximately 60% of treated patients. Cognitive symptoms and fatigue are frequently reported but may improve with ICP normalisation. The quality of life impact of IIH is substantial: work disability occurs in 30–40% of patients at 12 months. Bariatric surgery patients who achieve substantial weight loss have the best long-term outcomes, with remission rates of 75–90% at 2 years.
recoveryTimeline: Headache often improves within weeks of starting acetazolamide. Papilloedema reduction is detectable on OCT within 4–8 weeks of treatment. Visual field improvement follows over 3–6 months. Full remission with 15% weight loss typically occurs at 6–12 months. Without weight loss, most patients require ongoing medical treatment. Surgical interventions produce ICP normalisation within days.
complications: Permanent visual field loss — occurs in 10–25% of patients from chronic optic nerve compression; detected by serial Humphrey perimetry; prevented by prompt ICP control.
Permanent visual acuity loss — less common than field loss; occurs in approximately 5% of patients with fulminant or inadequately treated IIH; can progress to legal blindness.
Chronic daily headache — persists in approximately 50% of patients even after papilloedema resolution and ICP normalisation; requires specific headache management strategy.

CSF shunt complications — shunt failure requiring revision occurs in 40–60% of LP shunts over 5 years; complications include infection, positional headache, and over-drainage producing low-pressure headache.
Empty sella syndrome — pituitary hormone insufficiency in a small proportion of patients with severe empty sella from chronic raised ICP; pituitary function testing indicated if suspected.

Psychological comorbidity — depression and anxiety are documented in 60–70% of IIH patients from chronic pain, visual threat, and weight stigma; active mental health support improves overall outcomes.

supportResources.name: IIH UK
supportResources.description: UK patient charity providing information, peer support, research updates, and advocacy for people living with idiopathic intracranial hypertension.

supportResources.name: Intracranial Hypertension Research Foundation
supportResources.description: US nonprofit funding IIH research and supporting patients through education, peer networks, and clinical trial access information.

supportResources.name: NICE Guideline NG243 — Idiopathic Intracranial Hypertension
supportResources.description: UK evidence-based clinical guideline covering diagnosis, monitoring, and management of IIH including visual field protocols.

supportResources.name: American Academy of Ophthalmology — IIH Resource
supportResources.description: Patient-facing ophthalmology guidance on recognising and managing the visual threats of idiopathic intracranial hypertension.

confusedWithConditions.name: Migraine
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Migraine and IIH both produce chronic headache with photophobia and phonophobia. Migraine does not cause papilloedema, and CSF opening pressure is normal on LP. Pulsatile tinnitus and visual obscurations that increase with Valsalva are specific to IIH. IIH is frequently misdiagnosed as migraine, delaying treatment and allowing vision loss to develop.

confusedWithConditions.name: Cerebral Aneurysm
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: A ruptured cerebral aneurysm causes thunderclap headache and subarachnoid haemorrhage visible on CT and in CSF as xanthochromia. IIH causes chronic progressive headache with raised CSF opening pressure but clear CSF. CT and LP readily distinguish the two — both should be excluded when raised ICP is suspected.

confusedWithConditions.name: Pituitary Adenoma
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: A large pituitary macroadenoma can cause headache and visual field defects from optic chiasm compression. MRI clearly shows the pituitary mass. IIH produces an empty sella (not a mass) and bilateral optic disc swelling, while adenoma typically compresses the optic chiasm producing bitemporal hemianopia rather than arcuate scotomata.

confusedWithConditions.name: Multiple Sclerosis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: MS can cause optic neuritis — painful monocular visual loss with disc swelling — which can resemble early IIH papilloedema. MS optic neuritis is unilateral, painful, and associated with MRI white-matter lesions; IIH causes bilateral asymmetric papilloedema without focal brain lesions, and CSF shows elevated opening pressure with normal composition.

relatedConditions.name: Papilledema
relatedConditions.relevance: The primary ophthalmic manifestation of IIH — swelling of the optic disc from raised ICP transmitted along the optic nerve sheath; its resolution on treatment confirms ICP normalisation.

relatedConditions.name: Migraine
relatedConditions.relevance: Frequent diagnostic confusion and often comorbid; migraine headaches can become an independent secondary headache disorder that persists even after IIH remission.

relatedConditions.name: Glaucoma
relatedConditions.relevance: Both conditions threaten optic nerve function; glaucoma from raised intraocular pressure and IIH from raised intracranial pressure; their visual field defect patterns overlap, requiring both IOP and ICP measurement to distinguish.

relatedConditions.name: Cerebral Aneurysm
relatedConditions.relevance: Must be excluded by imaging when raised ICP presents acutely; also a differential for thunderclap headache that could mimic a severe IIH exacerbation.

relatedConditions.name: Pituitary Adenoma
relatedConditions.relevance: A key differential for new headache with visual field defects; MRI differentiates the pituitary mass of adenoma from the empty sella of chronic IIH.

faqs.question: What is idiopathic intracranial hypertension?
faqs.answer: Idiopathic intracranial hypertension (IIH) is a condition where pressure inside the skull is raised without a tumour, blood clot, or other identifiable cause. It mainly affects obese women of reproductive age, causing chronic headache, swelling of the optic nerves (papilloedema), and a risk of permanent visual loss if untreated.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of idiopathic intracranial hypertension?
faqs.answer: The main symptoms are chronic daily headache (often worse in the morning or with Valsalva), brief episodes of vision greying out (transient visual obscurations), pulsatile tinnitus synchronous with the heartbeat, and double vision. Visual field loss may be detected on formal testing before patients notice it.

faqs.question: Is idiopathic intracranial hypertension dangerous?
faqs.answer: Yes. Despite the historical term 'benign', IIH causes permanent visual loss in up to 25% of patients from chronic optic nerve compression. Without treatment, progressive field loss can lead to legal blindness. Early diagnosis and treatment with acetazolamide and weight loss substantially reduces this risk.

faqs.question: What causes idiopathic intracranial hypertension?
faqs.answer: The exact cause is unknown but obesity, female sex, and reproductive-age hormones are the strongest risk factors. Obesity in 90–94% of cases suggests elevated abdominal pressure or androgen-driven fluid retention impairs CSF absorption. Drug triggers include tetracycline antibiotics, isotretinoin (oral vitamin A for acne), and corticosteroid withdrawal.

faqs.question: How is idiopathic intracranial hypertension diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis requires a lumbar puncture showing opening pressure above 25 cmH2O with normal CSF composition, plus normal MRI brain to exclude tumours or venous sinus thrombosis. Ophthalmological examination detects papilloedema and visual field testing quantifies optic nerve damage.

faqs.question: What is the treatment for idiopathic intracranial hypertension?
faqs.answer: First-line treatment is acetazolamide 2,000 mg/day combined with dietary weight loss of at least 15% body weight. Acetazolamide reduces CSF production. Weight loss is the most effective long-term treatment and can induce remission. Surgical options (optic nerve sheath fenestration, VP shunt, venous sinus stenting) are used when vision deteriorates despite medication.

faqs.question: Can IIH cause permanent blindness?
faqs.answer: Yes. Permanent visual loss occurs in 10–25% of IIH patients from progressive optic nerve atrophy. Visual field loss is the main concern — it can be subtle and detected only by formal perimetry. Loss of visual acuity to legal blindness occurs in approximately 5% of inadequately treated patients. Regular monitoring and prompt treatment prevent most cases.

faqs.question: Does weight loss cure IIH?
faqs.answer: Weight loss of 15% body weight produces remission in approximately 25% of IIH patients at 12 months and substantially improves symptoms in most. Bariatric surgery produces remission in 75–90% of patients. Weight loss is the most effective long-term treatment; weight regain causes relapse in many patients.

faqs.question: What is pulsatile tinnitus in IIH?
faqs.answer: Pulsatile tinnitus in IIH is a whooshing sound synchronous with the heartbeat, heard in one or both ears. It is caused by turbulent blood flow in the dural venous sinuses under raised intracranial pressure. It is present in approximately 60% of IIH patients and often resolves with ICP-lowering treatment.

faqs.question: How is acetazolamide taken for IIH?
faqs.answer: Acetazolamide is started at 250–500 mg twice daily and uptitrated by 250 mg every 1–2 weeks to a target of 2,000 mg/day in divided doses. It is taken with food. Common side effects are tingling in fingers and toes (very common, not harmful), metallic taste in fizzy drinks, and fatigue. Blood electrolytes should be monitored every 3 months.

faqs.question: What is pseudotumor cerebri?
faqs.answer: Pseudotumor cerebri is an older term for idiopathic intracranial hypertension, meaning 'false brain tumour.' It was named because the symptoms of raised ICP — headache, papilloedema, sixth nerve palsy — mimic a brain tumour, but no tumour is present. The preferred current name is idiopathic intracranial hypertension.

faqs.question: Why does IIH mainly affect women?
faqs.answer: Over 90% of IIH patients are women, predominantly obese women of reproductive age. The sex disparity reflects the role of female hormones and adiposity in raising intracranial pressure. Research suggests androgens stimulate cerebrospinal fluid secretion via the choroid plexus, and obesity-related elevations in adipose androgen production may drive this process in susceptible women.

faqs.question: Can IIH occur without headache?
faqs.answer: Yes. Approximately 5–10% of IIH patients present with visual symptoms — transient obscurations, visual field loss — or pulsatile tinnitus as their primary or sole complaint, without significant headache. These patients remain at risk of visual loss and require the same monitoring and treatment as those with prominent headaches.

faqs.question: What is optic nerve sheath fenestration?
faqs.answer: Optic nerve sheath fenestration (ONSF) is a surgical procedure where small slits are cut in the sheath surrounding the optic nerve in the orbit to allow cerebrospinal fluid to drain away from the nerve. It protects vision by decompressing the optic nerve but does not lower global intracranial pressure or relieve headache.

faqs.question: Does IIH go away on its own?
faqs.answer: IIH can go into remission, especially with significant weight loss, but it frequently persists or recurs without active treatment. About 20–25% of patients achieve remission at one year. Persistent or relapsing disease affects 50–70% of patients and requires ongoing management to protect vision.

simpleName: Idiopathic intracranial hypertension (pseudotumor cerebri, IIH, benign intracranial hypertension)
symptomKeywords: whooshing sound in ear with heartbeat
swollen optic disc on eye exam
metaDescription: Idiopathic intracranial hypertension: raised brain pressure causing headache and vision loss in obese women. Acetazolamide, weight loss, and surgery reviewed.
